text: My port placement is very simple. If it's an obese versus thin patient. If it's a thin patient, the camera port goes below the umbilicus. If it's an obese patient, it goes above the umbilicus. The more obese, the higher up it goes. I place my right lateral port first. Usually there's a visual inspection of where the gallbladder and liver junction occur. About two centimeters below this give me enough access to retract superiorly. That's where I place this port. I then split the difference for the middle port. The left upper quadrant port, usually an eyeball in the general location of the middle port, kind of mirroring its location.

Port placement is very important as this can impede your progress throughout the case. Now, if you did have to place a system port for a very difficult gallbladder, especially in an obese male where you have to retract some of that fat or momentum purely, you can either place it between the lateral and middle port or middle and camera port, depending on adequate exposure.

The surgeon demonstrates dissection techniques, highlighting the removal or retraction of a lymph node to improve exposure of the cystic duct and cystic artery. A clip is placed on the lymph node, facilitating better visualization of these structures.

Of course, the goal is to get a critical view in every case and I will attempt to do so in every single case. However, occasionally it's just not possible or proceeding to blindly get that critical view can actually make it more harder or decrease your safety. I say this not necessarily to deviate from standard practice, but you know, there's not a hundred percent guarantee in everything. The goal is to get a critical view and do a safety section. And that's what I try to do in most every case.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to isolate and take the cystic artery first, enhancing exposure to the cystic duct and surrounding ducts for further dissection.

The surgeon uses a pro grasp to retract the liver, elevating it to allow the gallbladder to retract inferiorly. A dome down technique is employed from the top, providing clear access to the surgical plane. The vessels are visible, and dissection proceeds down to the mesentery pedicle, which will be clipped at the end.
The surgeon performs a close inspection of the gallbladder fossa using Firefly imaging, ensuring there is no visible bleeding or bile leakage.

The surgeon employs a dome down technique to dissect the gallbladder, with the cystic duct remaining as the last structure to be addressed. The use of ICG and Firefly imaging assists in visualizing the posterior wall and gallbladder junction, aiding in precise dissection.
